steps.details: Once signed in, click on the 'apps section' button in the sidebar to access the Microsoft Teams Apps section, dont click on three dot icon.

steps.title:
Within the apps section there will be 'Built for your org' on the left side under the search bar. If this 'Built for your org' is not found then please try to click on 'Get more apps' button. If 'Built for your org' is found then click on the 
'Built for your org' button.

steps.title: From the search results, look for 'Revo' app and click on the add button if it does'nt have open button. then after loaded click on open button to open its chat page.

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.8
Test Case Name: Help Command Redirecting Outside Teams
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure that the response to the "help" command keeps the user within the Microsoft Teams environment. Documentation and instructions must be delivered in‑app rather than forcing an external redirect. Bot responses may include hyperlinks for way‑forward guidance or contact; if hovering over such links shows a hand cursor, this is acceptable and is a PASS scenario for Non‑Integrated accounts.

Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:
Trigger Help:
Send help (or select Help from the command menu).
Analyze Response Format:
Confirm the bot replies inside Teams with a message/card.
Inspect Links (Updated):
Read the help response and classify it strictly into one of the below:

Way‑forward guidance / setup message (e.g., steps to connect account, configuration notes, how to proceed), or
In‑app workflow / list of actionable items / supported commands (buttons, CTAs, enumerated commands)
Then:


Detect if any hyperlink text exists and whether labels include external indicators (e.g., Open in Browser, View in Web Portal, ↗️).
Hover over the hyperlink(s) to confirm hand cursor behavior.

Verify Redirection:
Help must not rely solely on opening an external website as the primary action.
Check Canvas Usage:
If a canvas is opened, ensure it loads in Teams (Task Module or Tab), not a browser.
For Non‑Integrated accounts, sharing an external link as way‑forward guidance is valid.

Expected Result:

Help response must be delivered in‑app.
Either way‑forward guidance/setup is provided, or an in‑app workflow/list of actionables is present.
External links can appear as supplementary guidance.
Hyperlinks showing a hand cursor on hover are compliant.


Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL / WARNING]
Result Conditions:


✅ PASS

Step 1 or 2 is met:

The help response is way‑forward guidance/setup, or
The help response lists in‑app workflows/actionable items/supported commands,


and does not force the user outside Teams as the primary action.



❌ FAIL

The help response concludes by asking the user to do something outside Teams as the primary (or only) action (e.g., immediately opens a browser or only provides an external link without in‑app summary/workflow).



⚠️ WARNING

Step 1 or 2 qualifies for PASS, but the same help response contains a hyperlink text without an external indicator in its label.
Warning message:

“List of actions/supported commands/way‑forward guidance/configuration message contains a hyperlink without an external indicator. Kindly review the bot response.”



Failure/Warning Reasons (If applicable):
FAIL Example:
“Sending ‘help’ opened a browser immediately. Provide a help summary inside Teams.”
WARNING Example:
“List of actions/supported commands/way‑forward guidance/configuration message contains a hyperlink without an external indicator. Kindly review the bot response.”

Evidence:
[Insert screenshots showing the help response, the presence of actionables or guidance, any links, hover state (hand cursor), and any redirection behavior]

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.9
Test Case Name: Irrelevant, Improper, or Unresponsive Bot Responses
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Verify that the bot remains responsive and professional when faced with irrelevant, improper, or nonsensical user inputs. The bot must never remain silent and must avoid technical errors, raw code, or unhandled exception messages. For Non‑Integrated accounts, if the bot provides the same response for all commands but includes a way‑forward guidance message, this is a PASS scenario.
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:
Test Nonsensical Input: Type random characters (e.g., “asdfghjkl”) and confirm the bot acknowledges it.
Test Out‑of‑Scope Requests: Ask something the bot is not designed to handle (e.g., weather queries).
Test Improper Language: Send slang or unclear phrasing (within professional limits) and verify the bot maintains a polite fallback tone.
Check for Technical Leakage: Ensure no raw JSON, undefined variables, or empty bubbles appear.
Validate Acknowledgment: Confirm the bot uses a graceful fallback that guides the user to valid commands. For Non‑Integrated accounts, a repeated guidance response is acceptable.
Expected Result:
The bot must always acknowledge user input. A compliant response politely states that the input isn’t recognized and provides a way‑forward option. Silence or incomplete technical output is non‑compliant. For Non‑Integrated accounts, identical guidance for all commands qualifies as PASS.
Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: “The bot remained silent when the user typed ‘Hello bot’. Suggested correction: add a default fallback response.”
Example: “The bot returned ‘Error: Intent not found’ to an irrelevant query. Suggested correction: replace with a user‑friendly fallback.”
Evidence: [Insert screenshot of the bot failing to respond or displaying technical output]

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.10
Test Case Name: Separation of Welcome Message and Help Command
Objective:
To verify that the bot does not use the full “Welcome” message as its response to the “Help” command. The Welcome message is a one‑time onboarding event; the Help command must return a focused response with guidance, commands, or support links. For Non‑Integrated accounts, once the tool correctly starts the run, this scenario should be treated as a PASS.
Detailed Execution Steps:
Initial Installation: Install the bot and observe the proactive Welcome message (if shown).
Invoke Help: Type Help or select it from the menu in 1:1 chat.
Compare Responses: Compare the Help response with the original Welcome message.
Evaluate Focus: Check whether the Help response provides targeted guidance or simply repeats the Welcome card.
Identify Redundancy: If the Help response is identical or nearly identical to the Welcome message, the test fails—unless running under a Non‑Integrated account scenario.
Expected Result:
The Welcome message appears only once, during installation.
The Help command must return a distinct, focused response.
For Non‑Integrated accounts, once the tool correctly initiates the run, this scenario is considered PASS.
Final Execution Report:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.10
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons:
Example: “The bot returned the same Welcome card for every Help command. Suggested correction: Provide a shorter, task‑oriented Help message.”
User Impact: Users seeking assistance must re‑read onboarding content, reducing efficiency and cluttering the chat history.
Evidence: (Attach screenshot comparing the Welcome message and Help response.)

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.11
Test Case Name: Single Trigger Welcome Message Constraint
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
To verify that the bot’s onboarding logic is correctly isolated. The welcome message/Adaptive Card must appear only once during installation and must not reappear as a prefix or response to functional commands. For Non‑Integrated accounts, if the bot returns the same configuration-style message that includes a clear way‑forward guidance message, this is considered a PASS scenario.
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:
Initial Installation: Install the bot in the personal scope and confirm the initial proactive welcome message.
Command Execution: Send a functional command (e.g., status, settings, task).
Observation:
If the bot repeats the welcome message along with the requested data → Fail.
If the bot triggers the welcome message instead of executing the command → Fail.

Repetitive Testing: Send 2–3 different commands to ensure the welcome card does not appear again.
Evaluate for “Spam”: Confirm the bot does not repeatedly force onboarding material on the user. For Non‑Integrated accounts, a repeated message containing way‑forward guidance is acceptable.
Expected Result:
The welcome message must appear only once after installation.
Subsequent commands must respond with only the requested functional output.
For Non‑Integrated accounts, repeated configuration-style responses with way‑forward guidance are considered PASS.
Final Execution Report:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.11
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons:
Example: “The bot triggers the full Welcome Message every time a command is sent. Suggested correction: Implement proper state handling between onboarding and message events.”
Technical Note: The bot may be missing a state‑check separating membersAdded from message activities.
Evidence: Attach a screenshot.

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.12
Test Case Name: Welcome Message Content Integrity and Professionalism
Objective:
To verify that the welcome message content is high‑quality, relevant to the installation scope (Personal, Channel, or Group Chat), and professional. The message must provide clear value and instructions without repetition or irrelevant content. For Non‑Integrated accounts, once the tool correctly starts the run, this scenario should be considered a PASS.
Detailed Execution Steps:
Scope Verification: Install the bot in the relevant scope (e.g., Team Channel).
Content Audit: Review the text for irrelevance, repetition, and inappropriate tone.
Check “Who‑Installed” Context: In Channel scope, verify acknowledgment of the user who added the bot.
Feature Alignment: Confirm the message matches the app’s value proposition from the AppSource description.
Instructional Clarity: Ensure setup instructions and commands are clearly stated and easy to follow.
Expected Result:
The message is professional, concise, and appropriate for the scope.
It clearly states the value proposition, who installed the app (where applicable), setup steps, and available commands.
For Non‑Integrated accounts, the test should PASS once the tool starts correctly.
Final Execution Report:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.12
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons:
Example: “The message contains placeholder text and references unsupported features.”
Example: “Tone is unprofessional (e.g., slang or excessive emojis).”
Evidence: (Attach screenshot of the welcome message with highlighted issues.)

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.13
Test Case Name: Multiple Welcome Messages Prohibition
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
To verify that the bot triggers exactly one comprehensive message upon installation. The bot must not send multiple separate messages (e.g., greeting in one message, commands in another). For Non‑Integrated accounts, once the tool correctly starts the run, a repeated configuration-style message containing way‑forward guidance is considered a PASS scenario.
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:
Preparation: Ensure the app is fully uninstalled from the test environment.
Installation: Install the app.
Observation: Count how many distinct bot messages appear immediately after the “added” system notification.
Content Check: If multiple messages appear, assess whether they should have been merged into a single Adaptive Card.
Identify Failure:
Pass: One cohesive message or a single Adaptive Card.
Fail: Two or more messages sent in quick succession.
Non‑Integrated Exception: A repeated configuration-style message with way‑forward guidance is acceptable.

Expected Result:
The bot must trigger only one welcome message/card upon installation.
All introductory content (Value Prop, Commands, Links) must appear in that single message.
For Non‑Integrated accounts, a configuration-style message with guidance is treated as PASS.
Final Execution Report:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.13
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons:
Example: “The bot triggered three separate messages: a greeting, then an image, then a command list. These must be consolidated.”
Impact: Multiple messages create a spam‑like first impression for users.
Evidence: (Attach screenshot showing multiple bot messages.)

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.14
Test Case Name: App Name Accuracy in Welcome Message
Objective of this Test Case:
To ensure that the name used within the welcome message text (and the Adaptive Card header) matches the official App Name in the manifest and marketplace listing. This includes checking for typos, outdated names, or placeholder names. For Non‑Integrated accounts, the tool must sideload the package and search for the app under “Built for your org” and click Add/Open to start validation.
Detailed Execution Steps:
Check Manifest/Listing: Identify the official App Name (e.g., “ProjectFlow Pro”).
Install the App: Trigger the welcome message in personal or channel scope. For Non‑Integrated accounts, sideload the package and access it via Built for your org → Add/Open.
Cross‑Reference Names: Read the welcome message text and check for:
Mismatches (e.g., app says “ProjectFlow” but welcome message says “TaskMaster”).
Placeholders (e.g., “Welcome to [App Name]”).
Branding inconsistencies or unapproved abbreviations.

Case Sensitivity/Typos: Confirm capitalization and spelling exactly match the official name.
Expected Result:
The welcome message must clearly state the correct, official app name is Revo.
The name must match the manifest listing consistently throughout the message.
Final Execution Report:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.14
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons:
Example: “App is listed as ‘Invoicer Plus’ but welcome message says ‘Welcome to InvoiceHelper’. Developer must update branding.”
Impact: Incorrect naming creates user confusion and may cause security concerns.
Evidence: (Side‑by‑side screenshot of the App Name in Teams Sidebar vs. Welcome Message.)

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.15
Test Case Name: Prohibited Competitor Marketing in Welcome Message
Objective of this Test Case:
To ensure that the welcome message remains focused on the Microsoft ecosystem. The bot must not promote competitor platforms unless the reference is strictly for functional interoperability (e.g., importing content from Slack). Mentions of competitors used as marketing or promotional text must be considered non‑compliant.
Detailed Execution Steps:
Install the App: Trigger the proactive welcome message in personal or channel scope.
Scan for Competitor Keywords: Check for mentions such as Slack, Google Workspace/Google Chat, Zoom, Webex, Discord.
Evaluate Intent & Context:
Pass (Interoperability): e.g., “Use this bot to import your Slack archives into Teams.”
Fail (Marketing/Distraction): e.g., “Check out our Slack app too!” or “Better than Google Chat.”

Identify Ecosystem Diversion: Mark a failure if the message encourages users to leave Teams for a competitor without functional need.
Note: For Non‑Integrated accounts, once the tool correctly starts the run, standard validation applies.
Expected Result:
Competitor names must not appear unless necessary to describe functional interoperability.
The message must prioritize the Microsoft user experience and keep focus on the bot’s value in Teams.
Final Execution Report:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.15
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons:
Example: “Welcome message states: ‘Also available on Slack and Discord!’ This promotes competitors and is non‑compliant.”
Correction Suggestion: Remove competitor references unrelated to direct integration.
Evidence: (Attach screenshot of welcome message highlighting prohibited competitor mentions.)

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.16
Test Case Name: In-App Onboarding and Command Focus
Objective of this Test Case:
To ensure the welcome message acts as a functional “Quick Start” guide for the current app. It must encourage the user’s first in-app action and clearly list available commands, rather than promoting other Teams apps. For Non‑Integrated accounts, if the same configuration-style onboarding message contains clear way‑forward guidance and a contact link, this is considered a PASS scenario.
Detailed Execution Steps:
Trigger Welcome Message: Install the app and receive the proactive greeting.
Scan for App Redirection: Look for links/buttons encouraging users to download or switch to other Teams apps, or deep links to other app IDs.
Evaluate Task Nudging: Check whether the message encourages an immediate first action (e.g., “Create your first task…”).
Audit Command List: Ensure primary commands are briefly described in the welcome message.
Assess Interaction: Confirm the user can begin using the bot immediately based on the provided instructions. For Non‑Integrated accounts, a repeated configuration-style message with way‑forward guidance and a contact link is acceptable.
Expected Result:
The welcome message must not redirect users to other Teams apps.
It must include a first-action call to action and a concise command overview.
For Non‑Integrated accounts, the message is valid if it includes way‑forward guidance and a contact link.
Final Execution Report:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.16
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons:
Example: “Welcome message includes a button promoting a different Teams app and lacks command guidance.”
Required Correction: Remove external app redirects and include a ‘Getting Started’ instruction section.
Evidence: (Attach screenshot showing the prohibited redirect or missing commands.)

For Non‑Integrated accounts, if the same configuration-style onboarding message contains clear way‑forward guidance and a contact link, this is considered a PASS scenario.

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.17
Test Case Name: Marketplace Link Prohibition in Welcome Message
Objective of this Test Case:
To verify that the proactive welcome message is free of any hyperlinks, buttons, or QR codes that direct the user to any app marketplace. This prevents circular navigation back to AppSource and platform diversion to non‑Microsoft app stores.
Detailed Execution Steps:
Trigger Welcome Message: Install the app in the personal or channel scope to receive the onboarding message.
Inspect All Links & Buttons: Check every interactive element in the message.
Search for Prohibited Domains: Identify URLs pointing to:
appsource.microsoft.com
play.google.com
apps.apple.com
azuremarketplace.microsoft.com

Check for “Download App” Prompts: Look for text instructing the user to install a mobile app through store links.
QR Code Scan (if applicable): Ensure the QR code does not resolve to any marketplace listing.
Expected Result:
The welcome message must not contain links to AppSource or any other app store.
All links must lead to functional destinations such as a support page, privacy policy, or account sign‑up page.
Final Execution Report:

Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.17
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons:
Example: “The welcome message includes a ‘Download on the App Store’ button and an AppSource link. These must be removed.”
Correction Suggestion: Remove marketplace buttons. If users need a mobile companion app, redirect them to a developer landing page instead.
Evidence: (Attach screenshot of the welcome message showing prohibited marketplace links.)

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.2
Test Case Name: Proactive Welcome Message in Personal Scope
Objective:
To verify that the bot automatically triggers a single, comprehensive “Welcome” message upon installation in the personal scope. The message must educate the user on the bot’s value, provide onboarding steps, and list commands in an interactive format. For Non‑Integrated accounts, once the tool correctly starts the run, this scenario should be considered a PASS.
Detailed Execution Steps:
Install the App: Add the bot to the personal (1:1) scope.
Monitor Proactive Trigger: Confirm the bot sends a proactive message immediately without user input.
Check Frequency: Ensure the message triggers only once per installation.
Content Audit (Value Prop): Verify the welcome message clearly explains what the bot does and why it is useful.
Content Audit (Integration/Config): Ensure step‑by‑step setup or integration guidance is present.
Command Review: Confirm all supported bot commands are listed and briefly described.
Interaction Check: Verify the presence of an Adaptive Card with buttons such as “Sign‑up” or “Contact Us.”
Expected Result:
The bot sends exactly one proactive welcome message.
The message uses an Adaptive Card.
The message contains: Value Proposition, Configuration Steps, Command List, and a Sign‑up/Contact link.
For Non‑Integrated accounts, this scenario should PASS once the run initiates correctly.
Final Execution Report:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.2
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons:
Example: “The bot did not trigger a proactive message; it responded only after the user sent ‘Hello.’”
Example: “The message lacked a command list.”
Missing Elements: List missing components (e.g., missing Sign‑up link or configuration steps).
Evidence: (Screenshot showing the bot’s proactive message immediately after the system ‘added to chat’ notification.)

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.8
Test Case Name: Comprehensive Welcome Message Guidelines Compliance
Objective:
To verify that the bot’s welcome message is a high‑utility Adaptive Card containing the value proposition, configuration steps, command list, support links, and any required disclosures (such as limitations or notification‑only status). Bot responses may contain hyperlinks for contact or help documentation; if hovering over them changes the cursor to a hand icon, this indicates external navigation and is a PASS scenario.
Detailed Execution Steps:
Trigger Message: Install the app to receive the proactive welcome message.
Evaluate Format: Confirm the message uses an Adaptive Card with interactive buttons, not plain text.
Content Audit – Essential Elements:

Value Proposition: Check if it explains what the app does.
Sign‑up/Contact Link: Confirm a clear way‑forward action exists.
Help Documentation: Verify the presence of a user guide or FAQ link.
Commands: Ensure supported commands are listed with brief descriptions.


Content Audit – Disclosures:

Limitations: Confirm disclosure if an active subscription/account is required.
Bot Type: If notification‑only, confirm it clearly states non‑responsiveness.


Check for “Dead Ends”: Ensure the user knows exactly what to do next.
Expected Result:
The welcome message must be an Adaptive Card containing all required elements.
All links must be functional.
Required limitations or usage details must be clearly stated.
Hyperlinks showing a hand cursor on hover are compliant.
Final Execution Report:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.8
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons:
Example: “The message is missing the required disclosure about subscription requirements and does not include a Support link.”
Missing Components: List missing items (e.g., Value Prop, Command List, Notification‑only disclaimer).
Evidence: (Attach screenshot of Welcome Message showing missing/incorrect sections.)

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.9
Test Case Name: Proactive Triggering of Welcome Message (Personal Scope)
Objective of this Test Case:
To verify that the bot initiates communication proactively and automatically when added to the personal scope. The welcome message must appear without any user input. This ensures users are immediately aware of the bot’s presence and capabilities. For Non‑Integrated accounts, once the tool correctly starts the run, this scenario should be considered a PASS.
Detailed Execution Steps:
Environment Setup: Ensure the app is uninstalled from the user’s personal scope.
Installation: Navigate to the app store and install the app for Personal use.
Observation: Switch immediately to the bot’s 1:1 chat interface.
Verification of Trigger:

Pass Criteria: Bot sends a welcome message right after the system notification “User has added [App Name] to the chat.”
Fail Criteria: Chat remains silent until the user sends a message.


Failure Analysis: If the message appears only after the user types “Help,” classify it as reactive instead of proactive.
Expected Result:
The welcome message must trigger automatically upon installation.
The user should not need to prompt the bot to receive onboarding information.
For Non‑Integrated accounts, once the tool starts correctly, this test is PASS.
Final Execution Report:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.9
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons:
Example: “The bot failed to send a proactive message. It only responded after ‘Help’ was typed, making it reactive instead of proactive.”
Evidence: (Attach screenshot showing the proactive message immediately after the “added to chat” notification.)
steps.details:

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.6.2
Test Case Name: Message Consolidation and Anti-Flooding Check
Objective:
To verify that the bot consolidates its responses into a single, cohesive message rather than sending multiple messages in quick succession. This ensures users are not overwhelmed with multiple notifications and keeps the chat history organized. For Non‑Integrated accounts, if the bot responds with a single guidance message as its default behavior, this is considered a PASS scenario.
Detailed Execution Steps:
Trigger a Complex Response: Send a command that typically returns multi‑part information (e.g., “Get Full Status” or “Help”).
Monitor Notification Count: Observe the Teams activity feed and the chat window.
Count Message Bubbles: Count how many distinct messages the bot sends for that one query.
Evaluate Structure:
Fail: Bot sends multiple bubbles such as: “Hello” → “Here is your data” → a Table.
Pass: Bot sends ONE Adaptive Card containing all sections (greeting, data, table).

Assess Logic: Ensure the bot uses logical visual structure (containers, sections, dividers) within the single message. For Non‑Integrated accounts, if one consolidated way‑forward response is provided, it is valid.
Expected Result:
The bot must return the entire response in one single message.
The content should use Markdown or Adaptive Cards for readability.
For Non‑Integrated accounts, a single guidance message is acceptable and should PASS.
Final Execution Report:

Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.6.2
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons:
Example: “Bot sent four separate messages in 2 seconds for a single Status command. All content must be consolidated.”
User Impact: Multiple notifications cause “Notification Fatigue,” leading users to mute or uninstall the bot.
Evidence: (Screenshot showing multiple consecutive bot messages for one request.)

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.1.3
Test Case Name: Welcome Message Call‑to‑Action (CTA) Functionality

Objective:
To verify that the “Start Learning” button in the bot’s Welcome Message triggers the intended learning/onboarding workflow without errors or dead‑ends.
The bot must correctly start the learning experience and remain responsive in Personal Chat.
Updated Logic:

If no learning module/workflow exists after clicking the button (bot sends generic/non‑learning message), mark WARNING, not FAIL.
FAIL is only for actual bot errors, runtime issues, or dead‑end loops.
PASS when button works.
N/A when no Welcome Message exists.
NEW: If no actionable buttons/onboarding workflow is found OR any links are found in the Welcome Message, conclude the Welcome Message with a WARNING.


Detailed Execution Steps
Bot Deployment:
Install or reset the bot in 1:1 Personal Chat to trigger the Welcome Message.
Initial Interaction:
Confirm the Welcome Message appears and the “Start Learning” button is visible and properly rendered.
Trigger Action:
Click Start Learning.
Error Monitoring:
Check for:

“Bot Error Encountered”
“Something went wrong”
UI freeze or unresponsive behavior
➡️ These = FAIL

Learning Workflow Validation (Updated):
Observe what the bot does after clicking Start Learning:

If a learning module/onboarding flow begins → PASS
If button works but bot only sends a generic greeting / placeholder / non‑learning card / nothing meaningful → WARNING, not FAIL

Link/Actionability Check (NEW):

Inspect the Welcome Message for actionable buttons/onboarding workflow and links.
If no actionable buttons/onboarding workflow is found OR any links are present, mark WARNING and conclude the Welcome Message accordingly.

Payload Verification (Optional):
If available, inspect metadata to confirm the action ID maps to the workflow.

Expected Result

Clicking Start Learning must not produce an error.
A learning module/onboarding flow should begin.
Interaction should remain in Personal Chat.
If no module exists → WARNING
If no Welcome Message → N/A
If no actionable buttons/onboarding workflow or any links are present in the Welcome Message → WARNING (conclude the Welcome Message).


Final Execution Report
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.1.3
Test Case Result: PASS / FAIL / WARNING / N/A
Result Rules:
✅ PASS

Buttons in the Welcome Message are working.
Learning workflow or onboarding begins.
No errors shown.

❌ FAIL

Buttons in Welcome Message not working.
Runtime errors (“Bot Error Encountered”, “Something went wrong”).
Bot produces a dead‑end interaction.

⚠️ WARNING

Button works but no actionable learning module exists (generic/placeholder response), OR
No actionable buttons/onboarding workflow found in the Welcome Message, OR
Any links are present in the Welcome Message (conclude with WARNING).
Use one of these formats:

“Warning (No learning workflow found; kindly review the Welcome Message behavior).”
“Warning (No actionable buttons/onboarding workflow found in the Welcome Message; kindly review).”
“Warning (Links found in the Welcome Message; kindly review message content/labeling).”



🛈 N/A

No Welcome Message found after installation.
Use: “N/A (No welcome message found).”


Failure/Warning Reasons (If applicable):
FAIL Example:
“A ‘Bot Error Encountered’ message appears when clicking Start Learning. The educational workflow does not initiate.”
WARNING Examples:
“Start Learning button responded but no learning‑related workflow exists. Bot only displayed a generic message.”
“No actionable buttons/onboarding workflow found in the Welcome Message; kindly review.”
“Links found in the Welcome Message; kindly review message content/labeling.”

Corrective Action:

Investigate the Start Learning button handler.
Verify callback/action ID.
Ensure onboarding/learning content is linked correctly.
Add or correct actionable buttons and remove/label links appropriately if needed.


Evidence:
[Insert screenshot of Welcome Message, Start Learning behavior, errors/warnings, link presence/labels, or missing workflow]
Provide your feedback on BizChat
